questions.question: Explain the difference between image sampling and quantization with examples.
keyPoints: Sampling: dividing continuous image into discrete pixels (spatial discretization), determines resolution
Quantization: assigning discrete intensity values to pixels (amplitude discretization), determines gray levels
Example: Sampling a 10cm×10cm photo into 100×100 pixels; Quantizing each pixel to 256 levels (8-bit)

questions.question: Define and differentiate between 4-connectivity and 8-connectivity with a diagram description.
keyPoints: 4-connectivity: pixels sharing horizontal/vertical boundaries (N,S,E,W)
8-connectivity: includes all 8 neighboring pixels
4-connect used for simple paths, 8-connect for diagonal paths
Corner pixel has 3 neighbors in 4-connect, 8 neighbors in 8-connect for interior pixels

questions.question: Explain the RGB color model and state why it is suitable for display devices.
keyPoints: RGB is additive color model using Red, Green, Blue as primary colors
Colors formed by adding different intensities of R, G, B
Suitable for displays (CRT, LCD, LED) as they emit light
When R=G=B=255, produces white; R=G=B=0 produces black

questions.question: What is an image histogram? How can you determine if an image has good contrast from its histogram?
keyPoints: Histogram: graphical representation of intensity distribution
X-axis: intensity levels (0-255), Y-axis: frequency/pixel count
Good contrast: histogram spans full range with balanced distribution
Poor contrast: histogram clustered in narrow region (low contrast)

questions.question: Calculate Manhattan distance and Euclidean distance between pixels P(2,3) and Q(8,10).
keyPoints: Manhattan: sum of absolute differences (city-block)
Euclidean: straight-line distance

questions.question: List and briefly explain four common image file formats with their characteristics.
keyPoints: BMP: Bitmap - uncompressed, large file, lossless
JPEG: Joint Photographic Experts Group - lossy compression, good for photos
PNG: Portable Network Graphics - lossless, supports transparency
GIF: Graphics Interchange Format - 256 colors, supports animation

questions.question: Explain the concept of digital image formation. Discuss the processes of acquisition, sampling, and quantization in detail with their effects on image quality.
keyPoints: Digital image: discrete representation of continuous image as pixels
Image acquisition: sensor captures scene and converts to electrical signals
Sampling: discretizes spatial coordinates - more samples = higher resolution
Quantization: discretizes amplitude values - more levels = more gray shades
Effects: Sampling affects spatial resolution, quantization affects intensity resolution
Trade-off: Higher sampling + quantization = better quality but larger file size
Nyquist criterion: sampling frequency must be >2x highest frequency to avoid aliasing

questions.question: Discuss pixel relationships in detail. Explain neighborhood concepts, adjacency, and distance measures with mathematical formulas.
keyPoints: 4-neighborhood: N4(p) - horizontal/vertical neighbors
8-neighborhood: N8(p) - all 8 neighbors
Diagonal neighbors: ND(p) - 4 diagonal pixels
Adjacency: pixels are adjacent if they are neighbors and satisfy similarity criterion
4-adjacency: two pixels 4-connected
8-adjacency: two pixels 8-connected
m-adjacency: mixed adjacency to avoid ambiguity
Distance measures: Euclidean d(p,q)=√[(x1-x2)²+(y1-y2)²]
Manhattan: D4=|x1-x2|+|y1-y2|
Chebyshev: D8=max(|x1-x2|,|y1-y2|)

questions.question: Explain the fundamental elements of visual perception. How do human visual system characteristics affect digital image processing?
keyPoints: Human eye structure: cornea, lens, retina with rods and cones
Rod cells: brightness detection (scotopic vision)
Cone cells: color detection (photopic vision)
Weber's law: ΔI/I = constant (just noticeable difference)
Simultaneous contrast: perceived brightness affected by surroundings
Optical illusions: demonstrate brain interpretation limitations
Eye response to brightness: logarithmic ( Weber-Fechner law)
Spatial frequency: eye more sensitive to mid-frequencies
These characteristics influence: filter design, contrast enhancement, compression algorithms

questions.question: Explain different types of digital images. Discuss their characteristics, bit requirements, and applications.
keyPoints: Binary images: 1 bit/pixel (2 colors), used for documents, facsimiles
Grayscale: 8 bits/pixel (256 levels), medical imaging, black-white photos
Color images: 24 bits/pixel (RGB), 8 bits each channel, photographs, multimedia
Indexed color: palette-based, 8 bits with color lookup table, GIF format
Applications: Binary - OCR, fax; Grayscale - medical MRI, X-ray; Color - photography, TV
